2606.28510.title: Generative AI Literacy Training Improves Intelligence Analysts' Discrimination of Real and AI-Generated Images
2606.28510.paper_summary: Across social and online platforms, people are increasingly exposed to AI-generated images. As a consequence, the task of distinguishing AI-generated from authentic images is becoming a central challenge for information ecosystems. While humans perform better than chance, accuracy falls short of many operational needs. Initial evidence shows that visually oriented training can improve deepfake detection but does not improve participants' ability to identify real images as real. Here, we investigate the efficacy of a brief training intervention for intelligence analysts employed by the United States government in 2024. We conducted a counterbalanced within-subject randomized experiment in which we showed participants real and AI-generated images varying in pose complexity and scene context and asked them whether each image was real or AI-generated, both before and after an expert delivered a 30-minute training that pointed out patterns in seven real and 50 AI-generated images. We collected 2,544 image-level judgments from 32 intelligence analysts. We find training increased overall accuracy by 9 percentage points (95% CI: [2.7, 15.4]) from a baseline of 72%. We find the improvement is driven by a 14.2 percentage point increase in accuracy for real images (95% CI: [0.7, 27.7]). Through a careful experimental setup that curated matched pairs of real and AI-generated images across pose complexity categories, we reveal how these trainings influence people with different levels of digital forensics and generative AI experience and identify the kind of image-based content where this training intervention appears to be most effective. Ultimately, these results provide causal evidence that a brief, structured training can improve human judgment across a diverse array of real and AI-generated images, informing organizational responses to AI-generated visual misinformation.
2606.28510.paper_summary_zh: 在社交和線上平台上，人們越來越多地接觸到AI生成的圖像。因而，區分AI生成的圖像與真實圖像的任務正成為信息生態系統中的一個核心挑戰。雖然人類的表現優於隨機，但準確性仍然未能滿足許多操作需求。初步證據顯示，視覺導向的訓練可以改善深度偽造檢測，但並未提高參與者識別真實圖像為真實的能力。在此，我們調查了2024年美國政府雇用的情報分析師的簡短訓練介入的有效性。我們進行了一項平衡的受試者內隨機實驗，在該實驗中，我們向參與者展示了在姿勢複雜性和場景背景上變化的真實和AI生成的圖像，並詢問他們每幅圖像是真實的還是AI生成的，這一過程在專家進行了一次30分鐘的訓練之前和之後進行，該訓練指出了七幅真實圖像和50幅AI生成圖像中的模式。我們從32名情報分析師那裡收集了2,544個圖像級判斷。我們發現訓練使整體準確性提高了9個百分點（95% CI: [2.7, 15.4]），基線為72%。我們發現這一改善是由於真實圖像的準確性提高了14.2個百分點（95% CI: [0.7, 27.7]）。通過精心設計的實驗設置，策劃了在姿勢複雜性類別中匹配的真實和AI生成圖像的配對，我們揭示了這些訓練如何影響具有不同數字取證和生成AI經驗的人，並確定了這種訓練介入似乎最有效的圖像內容類型。最終，這些結果提供了因果證據，表明簡短的結構化訓練可以改善人類對多樣化真實和AI生成圖像的判斷，為組織對AI生成視覺虛假信息的反應提供了參考。
2606.28510.author: Negar Kamali et.al.
2606.28510.authors: Negar Kamali, Candice Rockell Gerstner, Jessica Hullman, Matthew Groh
